global.title: Open-Source Capitalism

coreIdeas.contextPhrase: The overarching philosophy — defines Powerhouse's bet that open-source can capture value, not just create it, by combining pro-market ethos with structured incentive mechanisms
First principle — marketplace platforms (like Uber/Airbnb but with open governance) optimize for liquidity and coordination between contributors, customers, and investors
Second principle — POWTs enable deferred compensation that links rewards to measurable contributions, solving the free-rider problem that plagues traditional OSS funding
Third principle — the RGH bridges decentralized networks and traditional markets by handling licensing, sales, and fiat revenue without compromising decentralized governance
Fourth principle — the OCF makes open-source investable by offering structured funding channels where returns are tied to downstream use of open infrastructure
The organizational corollary — Powerhouse encodes workflows in software rather than enforcing them through hierarchy, enabling independent teams to coordinate around shared goals

Historical context — DAOs failed due to coordination failures, incentive misalignment, and lack of execution clarity; Powerhouse emerged from this gap to add operational rigor to decentralized principles
DeFi accumulator pattern adapted for POWA token revenue distribution — per-token tracking without iteration
Epoch-based revenue distribution mechanics with weighted token supply proportional sharing
Architecture decision: on-chain contracts bridge to off-chain governance via the powa document model
Domain model for epoch-based token revenue sharing — the economic engine of open source capitalism

global.description: Economic philosophy combining pro-market OSS with sustainable incentive structures: marketplace platforms, POWTs, RGH, and investable OSS via OCF.
global.orientation: Open-Source Capitalism is Powerhouse's economic philosophy — the argument that open-source can be self-sustaining, investable, and scalable if paired with the right incentive structures. It rests on four principles: marketplace platforms for transparent coordination, Proof of Work Tokens for long-term contributor alignment, a Revenue Generating Hub for commercial monetization, and an Operational Collateral Fund that channels external investment into open infrastructure. This framework emerged from the failure of DAOs to deliver on decentralized coordination, and it motivates every architectural and organizational decision Powerhouse makes. Understanding OSC is prerequisite to understanding why SNOs, document models, and the two platforms exist.
